detected_item: Cabinet door hinge (standard overlay or European cup hinge)
problem_summary: Cabinet door is loose, sagging, or swinging freely. Most likely cause is a stripped screw hole or loose mounting screws — both are quick field repairs.
visible_observations: Door sags downward or sits crooked relative to adjacent doors
Hinge plate visibly wobbles when door is moved
Mounting screws appear backed out or turn without gripping

likely_causes.description: Stripped screw hole — wood fibers around screw are torn, screw cannot grip
likely_causes.evidence: Screw turns freely without tightening; no resistance felt when turning

likely_causes.description: Mounting screws simply loose — vibration or repeated use backed them out
likely_causes.evidence: Screws visibly backed out from surface; hinge wiggles but screws still have some grip

likely_causes.description: Worn or bent hinge plate — hinge is physically deformed
likely_causes.evidence: Door sags even after screws are fully tightened; hinge plate is visibly bent or cracked

clarifying_questions: When you turn the mounting screw, does it spin freely without gripping, or does it tighten with resistance?
Is the door sagging downward, pulling away from the frame, or swinging open on its own?
Is this a European cup hinge (circular hole in the door face) or a traditional flat strap hinge?
tools_needed: Phillips head screwdriver (matching screw head size)
Wooden toothpicks and wood glue (for stripped screw holes)
Replacement hinge — same model (only if hinge is bent)
next_best_test: Tighten all visible hinge screws with a screwdriver. If the door firms up and closes correctly, loose screws were the only problem. If a screw still spins freely, the hole is stripped.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: Tighten all hinge screws firmly with a screwdriver. Open and close the door to check if it sits correctly.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Door closes flush with even gaps on all sides — loose screws were the cause, repair complete.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: If a screw spins freely: remove it, dip 2–3 wooden toothpicks in wood glue, pack them into the hole, snap off flush. Let cure 30 minutes.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Do not skip the cure time — driving the screw into wet glue will strip the repair immediately.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: After curing, re-drive the screw. It should grip firmly with normal resistance.

step_by_step_repair.instruction: If the hinge plate is visibly bent: remove the door (support with a second person), unscrew the bent hinge, install a replacement of the same model, re-hang the door.
step_by_step_repair.warning: Cabinet doors can be heavier than they appear — use a second person or a prop to support the door weight.
step_by_step_repair.expected_outcome: Door hangs level and closes with even gaps. Adjust European hinge screws to fine-tune alignment if needed.

stop_conditions: Stop if the cabinet frame itself is cracked or split around the hinge area — screw reinforcement will not hold; the frame needs repair first.
Stop if the door material is particle board that has swollen or delaminated around the hinge — the material cannot hold a fastener and the door panel may need replacement.
prevention: Check and lightly tighten all cabinet hinge screws once per year as part of routine home maintenance.
Avoid hanging heavy items on cabinet doors — weight accelerates screw loosening and hinge wear.
Use the correct screw length specified for the hinge — too short strips the hole, too long splits the frame material.

retrieved_sources.title: Cabinet Hinge Repair and Replacement Guide
